questions.question: What is the difference between TypeScript's structural typing and nominal typing?

choices.a: Structural typing compares shapes (properties/methods); nominal typing compares names/identity — TypeScript uses structural typing
choices.b: Nominal typing compares shapes; structural typing compares names
choices.c: TypeScript uses nominal typing for class instances and structural typing for interfaces
choices.d: They are two names for the same concept in TypeScript

questions.explanation:
STRUCTURAL TYPING ('duck typing'): a type is compatible with another if it has at least the same shape:

```typescript
interface Point { x: number; y: number; }
interface Vector { x: number; y: number; }

const p: Point = { x: 1, y: 2 };
const v: Vector = p; // OK! Same shape → compatible in TypeScript
```

NOMINAL TYPING (Java, C#): types are only compatible if they explicitly declare the relationship (inherit from the same class, etc.). Even if two classes have identical shapes they are incompatible unless related.

TypeScript is structurally typed by design. Implications:
• Extra properties in an object literal trigger 'excess property checks' (a special case)
• Two unrelated classes with the same methods are mutually assignable
• Simulates nominal typing using branded types (see Advanced Types section)

This makes TypeScript very flexible for composable code but occasionally surprising.

questions.question: What are the key differences between TypeScript `enum` and `const enum`?

choices.a: They are identical; `const` is just a convention marker
choices.b: `const enum` is inlined at compile time (values replaced inline); regular `enum` emits an object at runtime
choices.c: `const enum` supports string values only; `enum` supports both
choices.d: `const enum` can be used across files; `enum` cannot

questions.question: How would you create a type-safe builder pattern in TypeScript?

choices.a: Use a class with all optional properties
choices.b: Use generics that track the 'completeness' of the builder state as a type parameter, erroring at compile time if required fields are missing
choices.c: Use a union of all possible builder states
choices.d: TypeScript cannot enforce required fields in builder patterns

questions.question: What is the `Readonly<T>` utility type and what does `Object.freeze()` have to do with it?

choices.a: `Readonly<T>` and `Object.freeze()` are equivalent — both prevent mutation
choices.b: `Readonly<T>` is compile-time only (no mutation via type system); `Object.freeze()` is runtime — TypeScript does NOT automatically narrow frozen objects to `Readonly<T>`
choices.d: `Readonly<T>` makes the object frozen at runtime

questions.explanation:
They address different layers:

`Readonly<T>` — COMPILE TIME:
```typescript
const config: Readonly<Config> = { port: 3000 };
config.port = 4000; // TypeScript error! (readonly)
// BUT: you can cast away Readonly at compile time
(config as Config).port = 4000; // TypeScript allows this
```

`Object.freeze()` — RUNTIME:
```typescript
const config = Object.freeze({ port: 3000 });
config.port = 4000; // silently fails in non-strict JS, throws in strict mode
```

Surprisingly, TypeScript's type for `Object.freeze(x)` IS `Readonly<T>` — so it does provide compile-time readonly when the return value is used. But `Readonly` alone doesn't freeze the object at runtime.

For deep safety: `Object.freeze()` is SHALLOW. Use libraries like `immer` for deep immutability.

questions.question: What makes TypeScript's type system Turing-complete and what are the practical implications?

choices.a: TypeScript is not Turing-complete — it has strict limits on type complexity
choices.b: Recursive conditional types, `infer`, and type-level programming enable computing any computable function at the type level — allowing extremely complex type logic but also infinite recursion
choices.c: Turing completeness refers only to the runtime JavaScript behaviour
choices.d: TypeScript becomes Turing-complete only with `declare` statements

questions.explanation:
TypeScript's type system is TURING COMPLETE — you can encode arbitrary computations using nested conditional types, `infer`, and recursive types.

This means:
• Type-level Fibonacci, sorting, parsing CSS/SQL/URL patterns — all possible in the type system alone
• Libraries like `ts-pattern`, `zod`, `prisma`, `trpc` implement sophisticated type inference that would be impossible in simpler type systems

Practical implications:
• Type complexity CAN cause TypeScript to hit recursion limits or take very long to check
• 'Instantiation is excessively deep' errors occur with deep recursive types
• This power enables libraries to infer return types from database schemas, parse route patterns for typed params, etc.

Guideline: use this power JUDICIOUSLY. Complex type machinery is hard to debug and can hide in library types.

questions.question: In a job interview, you're asked: 'When would you choose TypeScript over plain JavaScript?' What is the best answer?

choices.a: Always use TypeScript — JavaScript has no reason to exist anymore
choices.b: TypeScript adds value for: large codebases, teams, long-lived projects, complex data models, and cases where API contracts must be explicit; for quick scripts or tiny projects, the overhead may not be worth it
choices.c: TypeScript is only useful for React applications
choices.d: Use TypeScript only when working with decorators

questions.explanation:
TypeScript shines when:
• SCALE: large teams where multiple developers work on the same codebase — types serve as documentation and catch interface mismatches
• LONGEVITY: long-lived projects where understanding code months later is critical
• COMPLEX DATA MODELS: when your data has intricate shapes, relationships, and invariants
• REFACTORING: TypeScript makes large-scale refactors safe — rename a property and see all the places that break
• API CONTRACTS: when defining interfaces between services, libraries, or modules
• IDE EXPERIENCE: autocomplete, go-to-definition, and hover docs become dramatically better

JS might be preferred for:
• Very small scripts or utilities
• Rapid prototyping (though you can use TS loosely)
• Environments where a build step is burdensome

Key point: TypeScript's overhead is INVESTMENT — it pays off more as complexity grows.

questions.question: What is the `tsconfig.json` `strict` flag and what options does it enable?

choices.b: `strict: true` is a shortcut enabling a family of strict type-checking options including strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, strictPropertyInitialization, noImplicitAny, and more
choices.c: `strict: true` is deprecated; use individual flags instead
choices.d: `strict: true` makes TypeScript reject any use of `any`

questions.explanation:
`strict: true` in `tsconfig.json` enables a bundle of strict checks:

• `strictNullChecks`: `null`/`undefined` are their own distinct types
• `strictFunctionTypes`: parameter contravariance checking for function types
• `strictPropertyInitialization`: class properties must be initialized in the constructor
• `strictBindCallApply`: typed `.bind()`, `.call()`, `.apply()`
• `noImplicitAny`: error when TypeScript cannot infer a type and falls back to `any`
• `noImplicitThis`: error when `this` has an implicit `any` type
• `alwaysStrict`: emits `'use strict'` in every file
• `useUnknownInCatchVariables` (TS 4.4+): catch variable is `unknown`, not `any`

Always start new projects with `strict: true`. Never turn it off for 'easier' coding — you lose most of TypeScript's value.

Migrating existing JS to TS: enable strict flags incrementally.

questions.question: How does TypeScript help prevent common runtime errors compared to JavaScript?

choices.a: TypeScript adds runtime guards so errors crash loudly instead of silently
choices.b: TypeScript's static analysis catches type mismatches, null dereferences, missing properties, and incorrect function calls at compile time — before code runs
choices.c: TypeScript and JavaScript have identical error prevention capabilities
choices.d: TypeScript only prevents syntax errors, not logic errors
